speaker: interviewer
text: Now.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're on this one, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So now Schindler, we have to know a lot about Schindler-- when you saw him, when you spoke to him, what he said to you, how the other prisoners felt about Schindler. Did you pray? Now, remember in the movie-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] remember in the movie at the end, they showed--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Lynda Gonsenhauser. Today's date is May 26, 1996. I'm interviewing Anna Perl at her home in Virginia Beach, Virginia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anna, can you tell me about your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Anna Perl, and I was born Anna Duklauer. I was born in Szczawnica, Poland. It was a very nice little town. And was there seven mineral waters. And people from all over came. And they-- it was mineral water. And they said that the water was healthy. So with tuberculosis, asthma, people came there, mostly during the summer. It was very busy. Was a very nice place. But my father thought it's not big enough, and he can make better living in a bigger city. So we moved to Zakopane.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a tailor. But in Zakopane, he built a little business. It was souvenir business and leather. They-- they paint with colors the leather. And they make albums, wallets, and cover books. And this was his business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he make the leather items?

speaker: interviewee
text: He just cut out. And he has women, and they do this for him. But in 1939 broke out the war, September the 1st. And they came in, the German. And I don't know how they knew. They know where he has all this stuff, the leather stuff, and they took everything from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the Germans came, can you describe what your family life was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very nice. My parents, they'd been very good parents. Very good marriage, the best one I know. And my father, first he was a tailor. Then he was a tailor and the business he has. And my mother, she took care of us, we being three children, two girls and one boy. And when she had a little time, so she helped my father. She-- she-- in sewing, she could help him in everywhere. And this was life like until 1939, when the war break-- broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the level of your religious observance in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, she was a very religious woman. And she came from a very religious home. And every Friday evening, she light the candles. And High Holy Days we observed. My father, he wasn't religious. But it was freedom in our family. Everybody lived how-- how he want to live. And they didn't influence us, no father on his side, no mother on her side. Just they wait until we grow up and make up our minds. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, we went, but only on High Holy Days we went to synagogue. And afterward, I-- I did have a dilemma in believing. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war. When I lost everybody, I-- I have big problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war, what was your belief in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, I was 16. And I-- I-- I did after my mother, what my mother was believing, and the tradition, and these Jewish rules she was keeping. I and my sister, we went after her. We did the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your schooling before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I went to public school. Seven years I have in-- in Zakopane. First, in Szczawnica, five years, and then two years in-- in Zakopane. And then I start to learn how to sew from my father, to help him. And also with the souvenir stuff I help him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to evening school to-- to get some more education. And I went in this school until war broke out. When the war broke out, we couldn't go to school. Was not allowed. Before the war, did you belong to any youth organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. To Zionistic organizations I belonged. It was like Scouts, Jewish Scouts. And the-- the purpose was to-- to go to Palestine, to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- well, they try educate us and teach us Hebrew, alefbet and words. Not too much, but the basics. And they took us to the nature, and skiing, and to what else they-- was very, very nice leaders being there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the leaders?

speaker: interviewee
text: The main leader, his name was Moniek Spirra. And he emigrated to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moniek is Polish-- is Polish and is like Moshe. Moshe Spirra, S-P-I-R-RA. Spirra. I met him in Israel, and he still was very dedicated Zionist.

speaker: interviewer
text: You met him in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: After?

speaker: interviewee
text: After war.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he saved his life because before the war, he emigrated to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your siblings. What did they do before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were younger. My sister, she was three years younger. And my brother, he was '27, five years younger. And just they went to school. They'd been good students, nice people, good children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your town. Was it primarily Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was not very much. This was like 30 families-- Jewish families-- was there. And it was mixed. It was Conservatives, and some Orthodox Jews had been there. And everybody died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did everyone get along? Or did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated as Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: How we'd been treated? You could feel antisemitism there. You-- you could see sometimes they were waiting in front of Jew store. And they say, do not go in. This is a Jewish store. Or they broke windows sometimes. One time happened they went-- the older Jews praying early in the morning before High Holy Days. And they beat them up very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long before the war was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few years. Maybe two, three years before war it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you-- what did you and your family and perhaps your friends think about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew it was antisemitism there. And even small children, they went on the street when they saw us. And they say, [POLISH]. Jewish-- you're Jewish. You go to Palestine. You don't belong here. Stuff like this we experienced.

speaker: interviewer
text: On a regular basis?

speaker: interviewee
text: No regular basis. Sometimes happens. Sometimes. Polish people, they'd been a lot antisemitic. They didn't help in the war, [INAUDIBLE] somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said your mother came from a very religious family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her father, he died young. Was a tragedy. He went to-- to the woods, cut trees, and one tree killed him. And he was young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who? Who killed him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her father, a tree fell on him. And he was a very religious Jew, what I heard. I-- I even didn't know him. And my grandmother, [POINTS], here is on the picture. She wore a sheitel [INAUDIBLE] a week. She was so religious. So my mother, she-- she became-- she saw the tradition and everything. So she was, like, not as much. But she-- she was a religious woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's family were-- I don't know how was the family, but he wasn't religious at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did-- did you know your grandparents from your father's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- his father. I-- I knew his father. He was a teacher. And he teach German in school. And he was a very sweet, sweet man. I don't know his beliefs. I was-- I was small at this time. And I-- I don't know. But my father, he lost his mother when he was three years old. She died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then they put him to his aunt in Moravska Ostrava in Czech. And there he went to school. And he has a very hard life because they'd been bakers. And in morning, he has to get up, 5 o'clock, and take all the rolls and bread and distribute in the houses. And after that, he went to school, after three hours of hard work. Do you know how he and your mother met?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I know how they met. My mother-- in Szczawnica, where I was born, where the minerals water had been, so there my mother had a brother. And he went there. The-- the wife of the brother, her sister-in-law, she was cooking for people when it was a big season and the guests came.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was cooking lunches and dinners. And he was eating there, my-- my father. And my mother, she came to visit his-- his brother. And she was a very beautiful woman. You can tell on the picture. And he saw her in the mirror, and he fell in love right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and he-- he started to push her, because he asked her whether she has somebody. She said yeah. And he says, I am interested, but in three days I have to have the answer because I'm going to France. If you tell yes, so I stay here. So she said yes. And so in three days, they'd been engaged. And this was the best marriage what you can imagine. Never-- people not believe me, but never I heard an argument. Never in this marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your strongest memory of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have lots of memories. In what field do you mean? In what--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what the feeling was like in your home. You're saying it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just big love and respect and honesty is what I got from my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father-- did your parents-- when did they start to feel that things were changing for the Jews for the worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all-- all we start to feel when-- when the antisemitic stuff started. And we knew it's an influence-- from somewhere it came. For example, there was like Olympic Games in Zakopane in summer and in winter. And they came, the-- the Nazis and their spies. The spy came. And they knew everything what is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year this is we're talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was '37, '38, '39. If they came in-- in Zakopane, they occupied everything that was important.

speaker: interviewer
text: For example?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, airport and the-- the hotels, the beautiful hotels up in there. And the offices, they-- they right-- right away jump in and sit there. And then they say, not right away but later on, maybe around the new year-- this was September. Around the new year, they say, this is a beautiful city. We don't want Jews here. All Jews has to pack and go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was a very terrible moment for my parents. Three children, and right away pack and go? Go where to go? We didn't know. But we really packed, and my father rented a big truck, put everything in the truck. And we went to Cracow. Did he take the-- what did he take with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything we took with us, all belongings. The-- the [INAUDIBLE], he has the business. The souvenir they took from him. Before, I mentioned that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the date that this happened, when your family moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was around new year.

speaker: interviewer
text: The year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about when you arrived in Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived in Cracow, first thing was to rent some-- some room and stay somewhere. So in a Jewish very nice family, my father rented a room. And there we stayed as long as we could. And then they say it's not enough room and they don't-- they need the room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we rented in the outskirts of Cracow. We rented an apartment. And happened that this part where we rent the apartment became a ghetto. So then we don't have to move. We stayed in this ghetto. When did it become a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ghetto become probably '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did your father earn a living? How did you live before it became a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he was sewing. He-- he was a tailor. He was a very good tailor. Also for ladies, also for men. So always he finds job. And we-- I helped him. And then I found a job in sewing, and I was working too. So I helped.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother, she helped him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sisters and brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she-- they-- they went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: They went to school in Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were younger than I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when it became a ghetto, how did things change?

speaker: interviewee
text: It changed dramatically. We were closed. They put bricks around. Was like city in a city. You couldn't go out. You-- you have to have ID to go out and in each time, if somebody worked outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably they-- they were Ukrainian and German. Some Jews. Also some Jewish policemen they make from there. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many--

speaker: interviewee
text: But you have stores there where you can shop in the ghetto. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot. It was like a-- a part of Cracow. I suppose a big place. Many people. And then they started to make the selection and deportation from this ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: While the Jews were living inside the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they feeling and thinking about what was happening to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know. We didn't-- everybody lived his own life and started to make a living. And day by day, somehow they made it. And we would wait until the end if they not take us to concentration camp. Then they make the concentration camp in Cracow, Prussia. So we have to leave the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about the concentration camps while you were inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Because it was not-- it was a cemetery there. Jewish cemetery. Just they-- they create. It was no problem for them. So after-- they make a big deportation when they took my mother and my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know about the date this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I know. It was October, 1942. October 19. Yeah, never I saw them again, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was a terrible day. They-- first, they start to scream, the German, early in the morning, with the dogs. And then they say, everybody has to go out. And we went out. And there, when we went out, they make the selections. And old people on one side, the too young and children and-- with the old people. And healthy young people on the other side. So they went for staying, and, well, the small children and sick people, they went for going. And we knew they go to gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know about the gas chambers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew. We-- we knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did word get to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through-- from mouth to mouth. It come from the German to the Jewish policemen, and then it comes to the families. And we were in panic. It was rumors that there will be deportation. Was terrible fear there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were-- you knew a deportation would be happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your mother and your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother, he was blond with blue eyes. He didn't look like a Jew. And he wants to go out. And my mother and father, they'd been scared with a young boy, how he will survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: 14. So they didn't allow him to go. If they would allow him, maybe he wouldn't be alive. And then there was the selection, and they chose him because he was young, a little boy, 14. How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: 17. He was 14, younger three years than you. And they took him. And mother, she was so desperate. She ran after him. So I lost both of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your mother running after him. She knew where he was going?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't know. Just she ran. She knew she couldn't be without him. It was too painful. So-- so I lost both of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see them take them-- did you see your mother and brother leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because we were in another place. I was staying with my father and my sister. And they took him, and she ran after. I don't know where she ran.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in a line at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was behind a corner on the place where was the selection. So we stayed there. And then was quiet. And we went back to our-- to our apartment in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did they come for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't come for me. We worked in this factory. We'd been sewing the uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Optima. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sewing what uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the German. The uniforms we'd been sewing. All of-- all three of us had been working in there. No, and then they say they're going to liquidate the ghetto. There will be no ghetto no more. So who was there has to move to a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know about what date this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was in '42. 1942 year. And was-- it was fall, before winter. The fall. Autumn. And we-- what we could take, we took with us. Of course, not furniture, just what we could take in the hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what your living conditions were like during this time? You were with, again, your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And whom else were you living with?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister. And what is the question? In the concentration camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto, before they liquidated the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, before they liquidate?

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point in '42, what were your living conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have one room. And the kitchen was shared with three families. And it was-- was not too bad. We could exist there if they leave us. But they liquidate the ghetto, and everybody has to go to the concentration camp in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe this day when they came to liquidate the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was terrible day. Be without mother and brother, and now you have to go. Leave everything, just take something in your two hands and go to a concentration camp, which was built on a cemetery. So this was terrible, but what you can do?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you where they were taking you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they'd been talking. People had been talking. And the commandant was Amon Goeth. And we didn't know how terrible he is. But then we find out when we move in there. And when we came there, it was a cemetery, so they gave us--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they get you to the concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we walked there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not so far. It was not so far from the ghetto. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived, can you describe what that was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: They give us hammer in the hands and tell us to break the stones, the gravestones, and make street from it, roads, because it was dirty roads. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose gravestones were these?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Jewish cemetery there where they created the-- the concentration camp. And Amon Goeth, he was our commandant, and he'd been shooting people. So you can imagine what's-- how scary place it could get there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. How were you employed--

speaker: interviewer
text: --once you arrived at the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, we'd been breaking the stones. And then one group they took and told us we're going to a brush factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes? Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: To a brush factory. And we worked there in the brush-- I worked there in the brush factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of brushes?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kind of brushes. Cleaning dresses, coats, for shoes. All--

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's--

speaker: interviewee
text: All kind of brushes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this factory located?

speaker: interviewee
text: There in the-- in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the grounds.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But what was bad there, that there was window in there. And from Czechoslovakia they brought transports, and they'd been shooting the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we could see.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the window was there. Behind the window. First they have to put out all gold what they have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Put out? I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gold. Start with, like, gold and jewelry. And then they shoot them. They're from-- from Slovakia they came.

speaker: interviewer
text: As they got off?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from the trucks. And we have to see this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
